claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to gather some information about your circumstances and the children you're caring for.

Let me start with a basic question:

**How many children are you responsible for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible.

I have two children - Alex who is 6 years old, and Blake who is 3 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have two children - Alex (age 6) and Blake (age 3). That's helpful.

Now, to assess your eligibility, I need to understand your living situation:

**Do you live in the UK, and do both Alex and Blake live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK, and both Alex and Blake live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. That's an important part of the eligibility criteria.

Now I need to ask about your immigration status, as this can affect eligibility:

**What is your immigration status in the UK? For example, are you a British citizen, or do you have settled status, pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or something else?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, that simplifies things. As a British citizen living in the UK with your children, you meet the basic residency and citizenship requirements.

Now I have one more question to complete the eligibility assessment:

**Are both Alex and Blake currently in any of the following situations: hospital, care, or residential accommodation paid for by the local council?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake has been in hospital for a few months now, actually.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. That's important information. I need to understand Blake's hospital situation a bit better to determine how it affects eligibility:

**How many weeks has Blake been in hospital? Is it approaching or already over the 12-week limit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been 13 weeks now, so yes, it's over 12 weeks.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have one final question to complete my assessment:

**Are you regularly spending money on Blake while they're in hospital (for example, on visits, extras, or contributions to their care)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to afford to spend money on their needs while they're in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all of that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me explain the outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Summary:**

- **Alex (age 6):** ✓ **Eligible** - You can claim Child Benefit for Alex as they live with you in the UK and meet all the standard eligibility criteria.

- **Blake (age 3):** ✗ **Not currently eligible** - Because Blake has been in hospital for 13 weeks (over the 12-week limit), you are not eligible for Child Benefit unless you are regularly spending money on Blake's upkeep. Since you've indicated you haven't been able to afford this, the exception doesn't apply.

**Important note:** If Blake's situation changes - such as being discharged from hospital, or if you're able to make regular financial contributions towards their care - please contact HMRC to update your claim, as your eligibility may change.

Is there anything else I can help clarify about your Child Benefit eligibility?

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old and lives with you in the UK. You are a British citizen responsible for Alex and live with them. Alex is not in hospital, care, or residential accommodation. Therefore, you meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old and has been in hospital for 13 weeks. According to the rules, Child Benefit payments are usually affected when a child is in hospital for more than 12 weeks. After this 12-week limit, you are normally no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless an exception applies. One exception is if you regularly spend money on Blake (for example, visiting, extras, or contributions to their care). However, you have stated that you have not been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs while in hospital. Therefore, the exception does not apply, and you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, who is 6 years old and living with you in the UK. However, you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Blake, as Blake has been in hospital for 13 weeks (exceeding the 12-week limit) and you are not regularly making financial contributions to their upkeep. If Blake's circumstances change - for example, if they are discharged from hospital or if you are able to make regular financial contributions - you should contact HMRC to update your claim. Additionally, you may wish to explore what support might be available to help with Blake's needs while in hospital.

After this 12-week limit, you are normally no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless an exception applies. One exception is if you regularly spend money on Blake (for example, visiting, extras, or contributions to their care). However, you have stated that you have not been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs while in hospital. Therefore, the exception does not apply, and you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.

However, you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Blake, as Blake has been in hospital for 13 weeks (exceeding the 12-week limit) and you are not regularly making financial contributions to their upkeep. If Blake's circumstances change - for example, if they are discharged from hospital or if you are able to make regular financial contributions - you should contact HMRC to update your claim. Additionally, you may wish to explore what support might be available to help with Blake's needs while in hospital.
